coredata.dc:title: Anti-oil fouling poly(vinylidene fluoride) membrane with excellent underwater superoleophobicity and high-efficient photo-Fenton oxidation for oily water purification
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Lijing
Wang, Yi
Yang, Hao
Wang, Gang
Wang, Jianqiang
Zeng, Zhixiang
coredata.dc:description: Oil fouling makes serious trouble to the membranes for oil-in-water emulsion separation and purification. Here, an anti-oil fouling membrane was designed and constructed by loading β-FeOOH nanorods and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) on the poly(vinylidene fluoride)/poly(2-hydroxyethyl methacrylate) blend membrane. The fabricated BM@(β-FeOOH/CQDs) membrane can efficiently separate various oil-in-water emulsions. Furthermore, BM@(β-FeOOH/CQDs) exhibits stable underwater superoleophobicity with an underwater oil contact angle (UWOCA) of 152.5 ± 1.1° and a sliding angle (SA) of 2.2 ± 0.2°, which is beneficial to prevent oil droplets from contacting and adhering to the separation porous surface. Moreover, BM@(β-FeOOH/CQDs) has excellent photo-Fenton oxidation efficiency, which can photo-activate H2O2 to degrade oil droplets on the separation membrane. The flux recovery ratio of the membrane increases by 1.8 times when the photo-Fenton reaction was activated. This work provides an effective strategy for the engineering of anti-oil fouling membranes to purify oily wastewater.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Anti-oil fouling membrane
Photo-Fenton oxidation
Underwater superoleophobicity
Oil-in-water emulsions
Polymeric composites
Porous materials

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphology and chemical composition of the membranes
Oil-in-water emulsions separation and anti-oil fouling

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thank the support from the Ningbo Key R&D Plan ( 20222ZDYF020018 ) and the Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province ( LY21E030014 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) SEM images and photographs, (B) XRD patterns, (C) XPS wide scans, and (D, E) Fe 2p core-level spectra of the membranes.
(A) WCA in the air and (B, C) UWOCA of BM (a), BM@β-FeOOH (b), and BM@(β-FeOOH/CQDs) (c).

(A) Rejection and (B) flux of BM (a), BM@β-FeOOH (b), and BM@(β-FeOOH/CQDs) (c) for separation of the emulsions. (C) Photographs and optical microscopy images, (D) DLS curves of crude oil-in-water emulsion before and after being separated by the membranes.
(A) Protocol of the four-stage filtration test. (B) Flux and (C) anti-fouling parameters of BM@(β-FeOOH/CQDs). (D) EPR spectra of •OH generated in photo-Fenton reaction.

head.ce:title: Anti-oil fouling poly(vinylidene fluoride) membrane with excellent underwater superoleophobicity and high-efficient photo-Fenton oxidation for oily water purification

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Marine Materials and Related Technologies, Zhejiang Key Laboratory of Marine Materials and Protective Technologies, Ningbo Institute of Materials Technology and Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Ningbo 315201, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Marine Materials and Related Technologies
Zhejiang Key Laboratory of Marine Materials and Protective Technologies
Ningbo Institute of Materials Technology and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Marine Materials and Related Technologies, Zhejiang Key Laboratory of Marine Materials and Protective Technologies, Ningbo Institute of Materials Technology and Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Ningbo 315201, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Oil fouling makes serious trouble to the membranes for oil-in-water emulsion separation and purification. Here, an anti-oil fouling membrane was designed and constructed by loading β-FeOOH nanorods and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) on the poly(vinylidene fluoride)/poly(2-hydroxyethyl methacrylate) blend membrane. The fabricated BM@(β-FeOOH/CQDs) membrane can efficiently separate various oil-in-water emulsions. Furthermore, BM@(β-FeOOH/CQDs) exhibits stable underwater superoleophobicity with an underwater oil contact angle (UWOCA) of 152.5 ± 1.1° and a sliding angle (SA) of 2.2 ± 0.2°, which is beneficial to prevent oil droplets from contacting and adhering to the separation porous surface. Moreover, BM@(β-FeOOH/CQDs) has excellent photo-Fenton oxidation efficiency, which can photo-activate H2O2 to degrade oil droplets on the separation membrane. The flux recovery ratio of the membrane increases by 1.8 times when the photo-Fenton reaction was activated. This work provides an effective strategy for the engineering of anti-oil fouling membranes to purify oily wastewater.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Membrane technology has been increasingly adopted in the application of oily wastewater treatment. However, oil droplets tend to adsorb on the membranes due to their oleophilicity, electrostatic interactions, and rough surface, which results in a dramatic decline in fluxes. Oil fouling seriously troubles the separation processes of the membranes. Generally, a strong underwater superoleophobic surface can form a thick hydration layer and prevent the contact of oil droplets with the membrane matrix [1–3]. However, the barrier layer could be broken by the transmembrane pressure difference, leading to inevitable and irreversible oil fouling.

Fenton oxidation has been widely applied in wastewater treatment, which can mineralize many organic materials through the production of hydroxyl radicals (•OH) with an oxidation potential of 2.8 V [4,5]. β-FeOOH nanorods have been deposited on polymer membranes for mitigation of membrane fouling under the irradiation of visible light [6,7]. However, the photo-Fenton activity and utilization efficiency of hydrogen peroxide should be updated. In recent years, heterogeneous co-catalysts have been reported. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with a size smaller than 10 nm have been introduced into semiconductor photocatalysts to regulate the migration path of photogenerated electrons because of their impressive electron transfer and electron storage [8,9].

In this work, polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) composite membranes were designed and constructed by CQDs and β-FeOOH nanorods via in situ mineralization, which has excellent underwater superoleophobicity and photo-Fenton oxidation performances. Therefore, the porous surfaces not only restrain the adhesion of oils via the underwater superoleophobic mechanism but also efficiently degrade oils on the membranes via •OH free radicals generated by photo-activated H2O2. The outstanding anti-oil fouling endows the porous membranes with highly efficient separation for various oil-in-water emulsions.

As described in our previous work [10], the casting solution was synthesized by free radical polymerization of HEMA (5.50 wt%) in PVDF solution (11.00 wt%), and then it cast into blend membrane (BM for short) via vapor-induced phase separation (VIPS) process with an exposure time to water vapor of 3 min. Subsequently, BM was immersed in a mixture of ferric chloride (7.20 wt%) and CQDs powder (0.25 wt%) at pH2 and heated at 60 °C for 24 h. The resulting composite membrane was rinsed with deionized water and named BM@(β-FeOOH/CQDs). As a control, the sample without CQDs was also fabricated following the same process and called BM@β-FeOOH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and chemical composition of the membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The surface morphology of the membranes is shown in Fig. 1 A. Compared with BM, there are elliptical β-FeOOH nanorods on the surface of BM@β-FeOOH, and some of them have grown on the pore walls. As a result, white BM becomes reddish-brown after being surface modified. The shape of β-FeOOH nanorods did not change significantly after the addition of CQDs to the reaction solution, but their length grew slightly. The loading of β-FeOOH and β-FeOOH/CQDs on BM is 17.2 ± 1.1% and 19.3 ± 0.8%. XRD patterns of the membranes are described in Fig. 1B. Compared with BM, BM@β-FeOOH, and BM@(β-FeOOH/CQDs) have new peaks including 11.9, 26.9, 35.3, and 39.4°, which are the typical signals of β-FeOOH according to the standard card JCPDS No. 34–1266. The chemical compositions of the membranes were further investigated by XPS, the wide scans are shown in Fig. 1C. New Fe 2p peak can be observed from the spectra of BM@β-FeOOH and BM@(β-FeOOH/CQDs) in comparison to BM. And BM@(β-FeOOH/CQDs) has a weak N 1 s peak, which comes from the CQDs. Moreover, Fe 2p is fitted to Fe2+ (711.8 and 724.5 eV) and Fe3+ (713.1 and 726.7 eV) (Fig. 1D and 1E). The ratio of Fe2+ to Fe3+ ([Fe2+]/[Fe3+]) for BM@(β-FeOOH/CQDs) is higher than that of BM@β-FeOOH. The introduction of CQDs boosts the generation of Fe2+ in β-FeOOH nanorods.

Fig. 2 A shows the water contact angle (WCA) of the membranes in the air. It decreases from 116.2 ± 1.6 (BM) to 43.5 ± 0.8 (BM@β-FeOOH) and 15.8 ± 0.3° (BM@(β-FeOOH/CQDs)). The β-FeOOH nanorods and CQDs contain a large number of hydroxyl/amino groups, improving the hydrophilicity of the membrane. The underwater oil contact angle (UWOCA) of the membranes is described in Fig. 2B and 2C. UWOCA for BM is 148.0 ± 2.9°. After surface modification, it increases to 152.9 ± 1.2 (BM@β-FeOOH) and 152.5 ± 1.1° (BM@(β-FeOOH/CQDs)), and nearly unchanged in 100 s. Furthermore, the sliding angle (SA) of BM@β-FeOOH and BM@(β-FeOOH/CQDs) is less than 5°. They suggest that BM@β-FeOOH and BM@(β-FeOOH/CQDs) are underwater superoleophobic. After immersing in deionized water at 40 ℃ for 10 days, there are few changes in the UWOCA of the samples (Fig. S1A), and less than 5 ppm Fe leaked into the water (Fig. S1B). They suggest that these membranes have excellent stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Oil-in-water emulsions separation and anti-oil fouling
ce:section.ce:para: 10 g/L of various oil-in-water emulsions (lubricating oil-in-water, pump oil-in-water, and crude oil-in-water) were prepared using tween-80 as the stabilizer. Then they were separated by the membranes under 1 bar under the dead-end filtration mode [10]. Fig. 3 A shows that the oil rejections of the membranes are almost larger than 99%. The flux of these membranes gradually decreases in the order of lubricating oil-in-water emulsion, pump oil-in-water emulsion, and crude oil-in-water emulsion (Fig. 3B). It can be attributed to the decrease in particle diameter and the increase in the number of the emulsions (Fig. S2). In addition, the flux of the samples sharply decreases from BM to BM@β-FeOOH and BM@(β-FeOOH/CQDs) for the separation of various emulsions, due to the increasing denseness of the samples after surface deposition. For the separation of crude oil-in-water emulsion, the flux of the membranes is 439.8 ± 30.3 (BM), 323.3 ± 22.6 (BM@β-FeOOH), and 288.2 ± 11.8 L m−2h−1(BM@(β-FeOOH/CQDs)). Photographs and micrographs of the filtrates are given in Fig. 3C. It can be seen that the filtrates are clarified and no oil droplets can be observed in the micrographs. DLS curves of crude oil-in-water emulsion before and after filtration in Fig. 3D show that the filtrate particle size decreases continuously with the introduction of β-FeOOH nanorods and CQDs particles. These results indicate that β-FeOOH/CQDs coating decreases flux and increases separation efficiency.

A four-stage filtration test was carried out to measure the anti-oil (soybean oil) fouling performances of the membrane (Fig. 4 A) [11]. At each stage, the water flux was measured at 0.1 MPa and named J1, Je, J2, and JL, individually. The flux recovery ratio, reversible fouling ratio, and irreversible fouling ratio of the membrane after being cleaned by water were named FRR-W, Rr-W, and Rir-W, respectively. Furthermore, the membrane was continuously cleaned by H2O2 (10 mM, pH 2) under the illumination of a solar simulator (1 sun, 1 kW/m2), the obtained flux recovery ratio, reversible fouling ratio, and irreversible fouling ratio were called FRR-F, Rr-F, and Rir-F. The equations are also shown in the bottom plot in Fig. 4A. Four fluxes and anti-fouling parameters are shown in Fig. 4B and 4C. The total fouling ratio (Rt) is 79.5 ± 0.3% caused by the adsorption of soybean oil. FRR-W and FRR-F are 45.4 ± 0.4 and 81.9 ± 1.0%, respectively. In addition, Rr-W is smaller than Rr-F, while Rir-W is higher than Rir-F. Except for the underwater superoleophobic barrier layer, BM@(β-FeOOH/CQDs) can photo-activate H2O2 and generate •OH radicals that have been found by an electron-spin paramagnetic resonance spectrometer (EPR) [12] (Fig. 4D). These •OH radicals will degrade soybean oil droplets on the surface, leading to excellent anti-oil fouling performances.

In this work, porous PVDF/PHEMA membranes were prepared by VIPS and then loaded β-FeOOH nanorods by in situ mineralization with the assistance of CQDs. The addition of CQDs enlarges the β-FeOOH nanorods and increases the loading yield, which greatly improves the hydrophilicity and underwater superoleophobicity of the rough surface. BM@(β-FeOOH/CQDs) can separate various oil–water emulsions with a separation efficiency of more than 99% and exhibits excellent anti-oil fouling properties. FRR-F is 1.8 times FRR-W while Rir-F is one-third of Rir-W. They suggest that BM@(β-FeOOH/CQDs) can efficiently activate H2O2 under visible light irradiation to generate •OH radicals and degrade oil droplets on the membrane.
Lijing Zhu: Writing – review & editing. Yi Wang: . Hao Yang: . Gang Wang: Validation. Jianqiang Wang: . Zhixiang Zeng: Resources, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: Lijing Zhu reports financial support was provided by Ningbo Key R&D Plan. Lijing Zhu reports financial support was provided by Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the support from the Ningbo Key R&D Plan (20222ZDYF020018) and the Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province (LY21E030014).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2023.134684.
